---
title: "6 · Forecast of the soil state"
---

The twin is driven forward from a rainfall forecast to produce the **antecedent hydromechanical
state** — soil moisture, water table, near-surface stiffness — that the downstream hazard models
consume. The product is the antecedent state, **not** a flood hydrograph: converting the generated
runoff into a discharge hydrograph requires a surface-routing model, which is LandLab's role
(@sec-routing).

## Architecture: rainfall is a file, not an import

The AI weather model requires a GPU; the soil physics is pure NumPy and costs nothing. The two are
therefore separated by a **file boundary** rather than a function call:

```
Tillicum (H200)   FuXi  ──►  s3://gaia/soil-twin/forecast/fuxi/<init>.zarr
                                        │
anywhere (no GPU) ──────────────────────┴──►  water budget ──► θ, water table ──► Vs
```

`src/data/fetch_earth2studio.py` is the only module that imports earth2studio. Everything downstream
consumes a `ForecastForcing` (`src/models/forecast.py`) and is indifferent to whether the rain came
from an AI model, a reanalysis, or a scenario. Substituting the source changes one flag.

## The water budget

### Timestep

The budget integrates at the timestep of its forcing (`dt_days`). This is not a convenience: a
storm-scale forecast aggregated to a monthly mean loses the event signal it exists to provide, and a
month of rain applied as a single lump instantly oversaturates the column, generating spurious
saturation-excess runoff while starving recharge.

| 400 mm over one month | runoff | recharge |
|---|---|---|
| applied as one monthly lump | 200 mm (spurious) | 84 mm (starved) |
| daily, uniform | 0 mm | 322 mm |
| daily, 3-day atmospheric river | 54 mm (physical) | 305 mm |

Three parameters are **per-month rates** and are converted to the step: baseflow recession, capillary
rise, and gravity drainage.

::: {.callout-warning}
## A lumped monthly drainage fraction has no meaning at daily resolution
A gravity-drainage fraction of $0.6$ per month implies an e-folding timescale of
$30.44/\ln(2.5) \approx 33$ days. Gravity drainage above field capacity is a **1–3 day** process, so
propagating the monthly fraction to a daily step would drain the column roughly 15× too slowly.
Sub-monthly runs therefore use a physical drainage timescale (`DRAIN_TAU_DAYS = 2.0`) rather than a
converted monthly fraction. The two timesteps are not equivalent, and a calibration performed at one
does not transfer to the other.
:::

### Runoff generation

Runoff **generation** belongs to the budget — it is a sink, and omitting it biases θ and the water
table high. Runoff **routing** does not (@sec-routing).

The dominant humid-temperate mechanism is variable source area: rain cannot infiltrate where there is
no space between the water table and the surface.

$$\text{runoff} = \left[\;\text{liquid} - S_y \, d_{wt}\cdot 1000\;\right]_+$$

The response is continuous rather than a switch — at a 1 m water table, 224 mm of a 400 mm input runs
off while 167 mm still recharges — and it concentrates on shallow-water-table cells, which are the
valley floors and riparian corridors.

**Infiltration-excess (Hortonian) runoff is not represented** (issue #57), and does not fire at daily
resolution in any case: 68 mm/day is 2.8 mm/hr, far below these soils' $K_{sat}$ (15–55 mm/hr). Real
storms burst well above their daily mean, which is an argument for driving the budget at FuXi's native
**6-hourly** step rather than aggregating to daily.

### Lateral interflow

Water draining out of the root zone does not all reach the water table. On a slope it partitions
between a vertical velocity $\sim K_v$ and a downslope one $\sim K_h \tan\beta$; with the anisotropy
of layered forest soils, a large fraction leaves **downslope** as interflow and reaches a stream within
days.

$$f_{lat} = \frac{K_a \tan\beta}{1 + K_a \tan\beta}$$

Flat ground ($\tan\beta = 0$) gives $f_{lat}=0$: valley floors recharge, hillslopes shed. Without this
term the budget converts essentially all precipitation into groundwater recharge.

### Groundwater discharge to the stream network

The water table is bounded by the rivers. Groundwater standing above the local drainage flows to it,
and HAND is by construction the *Height Above Nearest Drainage*, so the head driving that discharge is
$[\text{HAND} - d_{wt}]_+$ and the table relaxes toward the drainage elevation.

This sink is **self-limiting**: the higher the table climbs above the river, the harder it drains, so
recharge cannot accumulate without bound. Valley floors (HAND ≈ 0) cannot rise; ridges can.

::: {.callout-note}
## The discharge coefficient is not free
A ridge water table genuinely sits tens of metres above the valley stream — a normal and *persistent*
state, sustained by continuous recharge down a long, slow flow path. An arbitrary discharge
coefficient drains that baseline away at metres per month. The coefficient is therefore anchored so
that the **observed baseline water table is a steady state**: at the long-term mean recharge $R_{ref}$,
discharge exactly balances recharge, and storms perturb around that equilibrium.

$$\text{gw\_recess}_i = \frac{R_{ref}/S_y}{[\,\text{HAND}_i - d_{0,i}\,]_+}$$
:::

Streamflow — the quantity a gauge measures — is therefore
$Q = \text{runoff} + \text{interflow} + \text{baseflow}$, and the water table balances
$S_y\,\mathrm{d}h = \text{recharge} - \text{baseflow}$.

### Snow: the recharge clock

Recharge in this domain is governed by **snowmelt, not rainfall**. Precipitation peaks in November;
the snowpack peaks in February; **melt release peaks in April — and so does the observed water
table**. The snowpack, not the unsaturated zone, is what lags the system. Melt delivers ~312 mm/yr
(≈20% of precipitation) in spring, when rainfall *and* ET are low, so an unusually large share of it
becomes recharge rather than quickflow or evaporation.

A degree-day partition holds precipitation as SWE until melt. Its parameters are calibrated against
**30 SNOTEL SWE stations** within the domain (881–1414 m), using each station's own precipitation and
temperature so that the 4 km rain/snow-line smearing of a gridded product does not enter the
calibration.

::: {.callout-important}
## Melt-out timing, not SWE RMSE
The calibration target is **when the snow leaves**, not the RMSE of the SWE series. RMSE is dominated
by the accumulation season, and minimising it selects a melt factor slow enough that the pack lingers
into June and melt release falls in May — a month later than the observed water-table peak. Fitting
melt-out timing recovers the April peak. *Fit the quantity the physics depends on.*
:::

Snow is also an **elevation-dependent** process, and the domain mean destroys it: the mean of a cold
mountain and a warm lowland is ~7 °C and forms no snow at all. The forcing must be spatially
distributed. Over this domain a January field spans −16.8 to +12.7 °C, with 23% of cells below
freezing.

### Water state → stiffness

θ and the water table map to velocity through the petrophysical coupling (Hertz–Mindlin contact
stiffness with van Genuchten suction, `src/models/dvv_coupling.py`) — a physical model, **not** a
fitted correlation. The Vs30 response is first-order travel-time weighted: a velocity change confined
to a vadose root zone of depth $d$ contributes $d/30$ of itself to the Vs30 change.

## The three states play different roles

::: {.callout-important}
## θ and dv/v are event detectors; the water table is an integrator
Soil moisture and the shallow dv/v band respond to **individual storms** within days and decay between
them. The water table **integrates** those storms into a single cumulative rise and carries the memory,
with no trace of their separateness.

**Vs30 barely moves** even when the moisture signal is large, because the vadose root zone is only
$d/30$ of the averaging depth: a 1 m root zone changing 6% in velocity moves Vs30 by ~0.2%.

**dv/v sees the storm; Vs30 mostly does not.** The observables answer different questions and must be
scored against different data — dv/v against event *timing*, wells against the cumulative *antecedent
state*. This is the argument for the seismic network, rather than the well network, as the instrument
for short-lead forecasting.
:::

![**The twin over a wet season** (western Cascades, 90 m, daily), driven by observed PRISM daily
rainfall. Soil moisture and the shallow dv/v band track individual storms; the water table integrates
them.](assets/twin_wetseason.gif){#fig-twin}

## Calibration and validation

The budget is constrained by **two independent classes of observation**, and the distinction is
essential:

- **Gauges constrain the fluxes.** Streamflow *is* the catchment water budget integrated over a basin,
  and a Lyne–Hollick baseflow separation splits it into quickflow (surface runoff + interflow) and
  baseflow (groundwater discharge). This is the only direct observation of the flux partition.
- **Wells constrain the state.** Depth to water, its seasonal amplitude, and its phase.

Wells alone are insufficient: the water table can be badly wrong while every field looks plausible,
because nothing in well data sees the flux partition directly. River discharge does.

Each parameter is pinned by a **different** observation, so the fit is constrained rather than tuned:

| parameter | constrained by |
|---|---|
| $S_y$ (specific yield) | water-table seasonal **amplitude** |
| $K_a$ (anisotropy) | **runoff coefficient** $Q/P$ |
| $R_{ref}$ (river-sink strength) | **baseflow index** |

Calibration is performed in **daily** mode — the mode in which the model forecasts — because the daily
and monthly formulations drain differently and a monthly fit does not transfer (issue #89).

| | model | observed |
|---|---|---|
| **baseflow index** | **0.52** | 0.47 (Lyne–Hollick separation, 6 gauges) |
| **runoff coefficient $Q/P$** | **0.71** | 0.57–0.73 (per-basin closure) |
| **seasonal amplitude** | **1.02 m** | 1.06 m (26,816 shallow-well observations) |
| water-table peak month | 5 | 4 |

![**The water budget closed against observations.** Left: the calibrated model against the two
independent observation classes — gauges constrain the fluxes, wells constrain the state. Centre: the
seasonal cycle, governed by the snowmelt clock — precipitation peaks in November, but melt and the
water table peak in April. Right: the flux partition.](assets/validation_domain.png){#fig-validation}

Comparison against wells is made **at the well locations**, not against a domain mean: wells are drilled
where people live and are not a random sample of the terrain.

::: {.callout-caution}
## Residuals, stated
The fitted $K_a = 2$ lies **below** the 10–100 literature range for forest-soil anisotropy. On steep
terrain a value of 20 drives $f_{lat}$ to ~0.82 — 82% of drainage shed laterally — which starves
recharge and drives $Q/P$ to 0.96. Either the literature range does not transfer to a 90 m cell, or
this single term absorbs other processes.

The water table peaks one month **late**. The direction is informative: an unsaturated travel-time lag
would make it *later still*, so that mechanism cannot explain the residual. Spin-up (a run initialised
from a baseline state) and the river-sink timescale are the likelier causes.
:::

## AI weather forcing: earth2studio

### Model selection

::: {.callout-important}
## `GraphCastOperational` emits `tp06` as a zero-filled placeholder
It is HRES-fine-tuned and does not predict precipitation at all; the `tp06` slot is filled with
`np.zeros` purely to keep the variable list consistent. It is also the *obvious* selection, being the
0.25° "operational" model. Choosing it yields a rainfall forecast that is **identically zero**, and the
water budget will forecast a drought with **no error raised**.

`assert_precipitation_is_real()` refuses it by name and refuses any all-zero field, pinned by a test.
:::

Across earth2studio's prognostic models, **FuXi is the only other model that natively emits `tp06`**.
Pangu, FengWu, Aurora, SFNO, FCN3 and ACE2 emit no precipitation at all and require the
`PrecipitationAFNO` diagnostic to be chained on.

| | GraphCastSmall | **FuXi** (selected) |
|---|---|---|
| precipitation | `tp06` | `tp06` |
| grid | 1° (~110×75 km) | **0.25°** (~28×19 km) |
| horizon | ~10 d | **cascade trained for 5 / 10 / 15 d** |
| runtime dependency | `jax[cuda13]` → **GPU-only** | **onnxruntime** |

FuXi covers the +1…+15 day horizon by design, at 4× the resolution, and is not CUDA-JAX-locked.

### Execution

GraphCast cannot run on macOS/arm64 at all: its earth2studio extra requires `jax[cuda13]`. FuXi is
onnxruntime-based and imports on CPU/arm64, but its weights are **9.43 GB** (a three-model cascade) and
CPU inference over 60 six-hourly steps is impractical. It is a cluster job.

Forecasts are generated on **Hyak Tillicum (H200)** via SkyPilot (`deploy/tillicum-fuxi.yaml`),
following `gaia-hazlab/skypilot-hyak` conventions:

```bash
pixi run stage-mvp                 # reports which initialisations are missing from Kopah
sky launch --infra slurm/tillicum -c fuxi deploy/tillicum-fuxi.yaml \
  --env INITS="2025-09-01,2025-09-08,..." \
  --env AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID --env AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY
```

A rolling **weekly initialisation** across the wet season yields ~29 forecasts, each verified over its
own +1…+15 day lead. This is what permits forecast skill to be measured **as a function of lead time**
rather than inferred from a single run. The weight cascade is pulled once and cached, so the sweep runs
as one job. Slurm nodes do not autostop, and `time` is a hard kill: it must be sized for the whole
sweep.

### Known limits of the adapter

The `tp06` **unit convention** (accumulated metres versus millimetres) differs between checkpoints and
is guarded by a magnitude check that fails loudly rather than guessing. The 0.25° grid
**under-resolves the Cascade crest**, so orographic enhancement must be bias-corrected against PRISM
before precipitation totals are trusted.

## Data staging: Zarr on Kopah

Staging follows `gaia-hazlab/gaia-cli` so that the stores interoperate: obstore +
`zarr.storage.ObjectStore`, `zarr_format=3`, `consolidated=False`, ~10 MB chunks, credentials from
`AWS_PROFILE`, and the Kopah endpoint via `AWS_ENDPOINT_URL`.

```
s3://gaia/soil-twin/static/svm_cvm17.zarr        SVM shallow-Vs grid
s3://gaia/soil-twin/static/vs30_90m.zarr         derived Vs30
s3://gaia/soil-twin/forecast/fuxi/<init>.zarr    AI rainfall forcing
```

The USGS CVM17 SVM grid is distributed as a **6.2 GB netCDF that must be downloaded in full** — and is
captcha-gated, so it cannot be scripted — before a single Vs30 value can be read. Staged as chunked
Zarr it is **756 MB** and is read lazily: only the analysis window, only the shallow levels. Vs30
re-derived from the Zarr is bit-identical to the netCDF-derived field. Chunks are 256×256 spatial tiles
× **full depth**, because every read is "a window, all depths" — the shape of the travel-time integral.

::: {.callout-note}
## Two staging constraints
SkyPilot `file_mounts` do not work with Kopah's custom endpoint; the S3 API is used directly rather
than mounting. And a chunking helper that overrides a caller's dask chunks makes one Zarr chunk span
several dask chunks — xarray refuses this outright, because writing it in parallel can corrupt data.
:::

## Observed forcing: PRISM daily

`src/data/fetch_prism_daily.py` reads the `gaia-hazlab/prism-stac` catalog via `odc.stac`, falling back
to the PRISM service directly for windows the catalog does not cover. It also pulls `tmean` (required
by the snow partition) and adds Hamon PET per day.

Observed forcing serves two distinct purposes: a **hindcast** — driving the soil state with what
actually fell, a perfect-forcing baseline that isolates the physics from weather-forecast error — and
the **verification truth** against which FuXi's precipitation is scored.

```bash
pixi run stage-mvp        # forcing + state + flux for the analysis window
pixi run hindcast         # observed-forcing hindcast on the 90 m grid
pixi run forecast-leadtime --forcing-zarr s3://gaia/soil-twin/forecast/fuxi/<init>.zarr
```

## Scope: antecedent state, not hydrograph {#sec-routing}

The budget **generates** runoff and interflow as source terms; it does not route them. Converting those
into a discharge hydrograph requires a surface-routing model, which is LandLab's role (issue #55), and
the source terms are exported to it.

The value of the antecedent state is that a saturated subsurface — a water table already raised by a
winter atmospheric-river sequence — is what turns the *next* storm into a landslide or a
liquefaction-susceptible condition. That is the state the twin forecasts.
